coredata.dc:title: The influence of inorganic electrolyte on the properties of carbon quantum dots in electrochemical exfoliation
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Lijiang
Chen, Fulong
Ding, Hui
Li, Xuehua
Li, Xiaobing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with highly graphitic structure are of great importance for electronic performance in applications of optoelectronics or photocatalysis. A facile method to obtain CQDs is the electrochemical exfoliation of graphitic materials. In our previous work, CQDs have been exfoliated from graphite rods in electrolyte of distilled water, organic and inorganic solutions, and the results show that inorganic electrolyte is the best choice in economy and efficiency. According to previous conclusions, the type of electrolyte has a prominent influence on the property and morphology of CQDs in process of exfoliation. However, to the best our knowledge, the type of inorganic electrolyte on behaviors of CQDs has not been researched and reported. Herein, a series of common inorganic salt solution are used as electrolyte and are compared to each other for the best choice of electrochemical exfoliation. Nitrate solution for electrochemical exfoliation of CQDs is better than any others, not only due to its similar photoluminescence, but also possessing common average size of 3.5 nm, which lead to regular and easily distinguishing morphology in TEM image. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm observed from HRTEM is indicative of graphitic nanostructure. The blue photoluminescence of as-exfoliated CQDs is relative weak with quantum yield of 5.6%. Thus, we are inclined to believe that its photoluminescence mechanism is attribute to quantum confinement effects of carbon core rather than surface functional groups. It can also be supported by XPS and FT-IR results that only some simple groups (-OH and C=O) were form on surface of CQDs during exfoliation. Therefore, the electrolyte of nitrate shows the best suitability for the electrochemical exfoliation of CQDs. Besides, it can provide a way to tune the size of CQDs by electing different electrolyte.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Electrochemical exfoliation
Inorganic salt-assisted
Nitrate

Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CQDs electrochemically exfoliated from graphite rods in 0.1 mol/L NaNO3 and using 15 V voltage. (b) HRTEM image of the CQDs. (c) The size distribution histogram and fitted Gaussian curve of the CQDs.

TEM images of the exfoliated CQDs in electrolyte concentration of 0.1 mol/L (a) NaCl, (b) NaNO2, (c) Na2CO3 and (d) KNO3, respectively. Each inset image is the size distribution histogram and fitted Gaussian curve of the corresponding CQDs.
UV–visible absorption spectrum (left) and PL emission spectra (right) of the CQDs exfoliated in different inorganic solution (a) NaNO3, (b) NaNO2, (c) NaCO3 and (d) KNO3, respectively.
(a) Survey of XPS spectrum of the as-exfoliated CQDs, the high-resolution of XPS spectra of C 1 s (b) and O 1 s (c), (d) FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs.

TEM images and corresponding size distribution histogram & fitted Gaussian curve of the resultant CQDs exfoliated in different concentration electrolyte of nitrate, (a) 0.05 mol/L, (b) 0.1 mol/L and (c) 0.5 mol/L.

head.ce:title: The influence of inorganic electrolyte on the properties of carbon quantum dots in electrochemical exfoliation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China University of Mining and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with highly graphitic structure are of great importance for electronic performance in applications of optoelectronics or photocatalysis. A facile method to obtain CQDs is the electrochemical exfoliation of graphitic materials. In our previous work, CQDs have been exfoliated from graphite rods in electrolyte of distilled water, organic and inorganic solutions, and the results show that inorganic electrolyte is the best choice in economy and efficiency. According to previous conclusions, the type of electrolyte has a prominent influence on the property and morphology of CQDs in process of exfoliation. However, to the best our knowledge, the type of inorganic electrolyte on behaviors of CQDs has not been researched and reported. Herein, a series of common inorganic salt solution are used as electrolyte and are compared to each other for the best choice of electrochemical exfoliation. Nitrate solution for electrochemical exfoliation of CQDs is better than any others, not only due to its similar photoluminescence, but also possessing common average size of 3.5 nm, which lead to regular and easily distinguishing morphology in TEM image. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm observed from HRTEM is indicative of graphitic nanostructure. The blue photoluminescence of as-exfoliated CQDs is relative weak with quantum yield of 5.6%. Thus, we are inclined to believe that its photoluminescence mechanism is attribute to quantum confinement effects of carbon core rather than surface functional groups. It can also be supported by XPS and FT-IR results that only some simple groups (-OH and C=O) were form on surface of CQDs during exfoliation. Therefore, the electrolyte of nitrate shows the best suitability for the electrochemical exfoliation of CQDs. Besides, it can provide a way to tune the size of CQDs by electing different electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), known as carbon dots with prominently graphitic crystalline structure, are of particular interest as photocatalytic or electronic nanomaterials due to excellent electron reservoir, good electronic transmission, and emerging light-absorbing properties [1–4]. These properties directly increase the efficiency of hole scavenging by the electron-donor and thereby significantly extending the lifetime of the photogenerated electrons, which further improve performance of the devices [5–11]. Thus, the CQDs with graphitic structure is therefore highly desirable by different methods. Generally, the synthesis of CQDs can be classified into top-down and bottom-up method [7]. Bottom-up, represented by hydrothermal method, predominantly involve the thermal decomposition and further carbonization of cheap organic molecular precursors [12], which can obtain CQDs with different compositions ranging from amorphous to graphitic CQDs depending on temperature employed. Temperature blow 300 °C usually leads to amorphous carbon dots, while higher temperatures are responsible for graphitic CQDs [13]. Nevertheless, hydrothermal graphitization process requires special and expensive technique to meet the strict conditions of high pressure and high temperature. Top-down methods refer to cutting carbon or graphite bulk materials (carbon-based precursors) into nanoparticles by arc discharge [14], electron beam lithography [15], laser ablation [16], and electrochemical exfoliation [17–19]. The as-synthesized CQDs usually are superior to ones by bottom-up methods on graphitization due to inheriting crystalline structure of precursor experienced high-temperature formation or graphitization process (>1000 °C). Therefore, top-down approaches are efficient routes to obtain CQDs with graphitic structure.

Comparing to other top-down methods, electrochemical exfoliation not only avoid using strong acids [20,21] (such as H2SO4 and HNO3) to realize the oxidation and cutting precursor, but also utilize common apparatus with simple operations and without harsh reaction conditions. Therefore, electrochemical exfoliation is considered to be the most promising methods for facile, green and large-scale preparation of CQDs [22]. Some studies have demonstrated that the electrolyte environment played an important role in the exfoliation of CQDs [23], but more electrolytes are organic solvents, such as ionic liquids [24], sodium methoxide [25], sodium p-toluenesulfonate [26], alcohol/NaOH [27] and so on. It is well known that both intercalation-exfoliation and oxidation-cutting effects appeared in the anodizing process [17,24]. The larger size of organic molecules than inorganic ones facilitate intercalation-exfoliation process, causing graphite flakes and sheets to separate from the anode, and the detached parts needed to be further oxidized or cut for small CQDs. Besides, organic electrolyte also faces limitations or problems such as the residue on surface of CQDs, high cost and difficult separation. Thus, it is a feasible way for electrochemical method to utilize inorganic solution instead of organic solvent, and we have electrochemically exfoliated small CQDs in the electrolyte of common salt with low cost and high efficiency [28]. But, due to weak oxidizing and corrosive (cutting) ability of salt, the obtained CQDs with smaller average size of 1.7 nm is obviously different from usual CQDs with size of 3–5 nm [28], leading to difficult distinguishing and separation. Besides, to best our known, there are no investigation about the influence of inorganic salts' type on morphology and photoluminescence (PL) of CQDs. Therefore, it is necessary to search proper inorganic salt as electrochemical electrolyte for CQDs with normal size and higher regular on morphology. It is also of great significance to rich investigation of electrochemical exfoliation and to provide more support for the requirement of deepening researches and optoelectronic application as well.

Herein, some typical inorganic salts, including nitrite, chloride, nitrate, bicarbonate, and phosphate, were selected as electrolyte for electrochemical exfoliation of CQDs, which were compared to each other on the morphology and optical properties. The results indicate that the CQDs exfoliated in nitrate have larger average size (3.4 nm) and wider size distribution than other inorganic electrolytes, leading to the CQDs displaying more regular morphology in TEM images. The CQDs exfoliated in different electrolyte possess similar PL behaviors of excitation-dependent redshift and weak blue emission, which is mainly attributed to quantum confinement effect from carbon core. Besides, the simple surface functional groups on CQDs, according to the results of XPS and FTIR, provide indirect evidence of carbon core emission as well. It can be summarized that nitrate salt may be optimal choice in inorganic salt as the electrolyte, because the as-prepared CQDs not only show similar PL behaviors but also possess bigger size than others. This work may be able to provide a practical guide for the synthesis of CQDs using electrochemical exfoliation method, owing to cheap and easy-access raw material, plain equipment and convenient operation, and shorter consuming time. This new strategy is of great significance to meet potentially huge requirement of CQDs with better graphitic structure for the application of catalysis or electronic devices.

The electrochemical exfoliation of CQDs was carried out in a two-electrode cell configuration, in which two graphite rods (0.6 cm in diameter and 10 cm in length) are adopted as anode and cathode, respectively. In a typical experiment, a suitable amount of inorganic salts (including sodium nitrate, potassium nitrate, sodium nitrite, sodium carbonate, and sodium hydrogen phosphate, respectively) was dissolved in distilled water to obtain 0.1 mol/L solution as electrolyte. The rods were rinsed by ethanol and distilled water via ultrasonic treatment in turn and were vertically inserted into the electrolyte in parallel with distance of 2 cm. Static potential of 15 V was applied to the electrodes using a direct current power supply and a magnetic stirrer was continuously working during whole exfoliation. As time proceed, the anode rods began gradually being corroded and the electrolyte changed from colorless solution to yellow and then to dark-brown. The as-exfoliated CQDs were present in resulting solution. The solution was filtrated and centrifugated to remove the precipitated graphite deposit. Finally, the obtained supernatant was CQDs aqueous solution, which can be further freeze dried to prepare solid state CQDs. Other CQDs were exfoliated in different electrolyte, including chloride, nitrite, bicarbonate and hydrogen phosphate, which keeping other conditions constant.

The images of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were obtained on a JEM-2100 electron microscope (Jeol, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The test sample was prepared by drop-casting the aqueous solution on a carbon-coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. The size and distribution of CQDs were measured by Nano Measurer (software) based on obtained TEM images, guaranteeing that statistical account was no less than 200 for each sample in one or more images. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out on X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (PHI 5000 Versa Probe, UIVAC-PHI, Japan). The test sample was freeze dried CQDs putting on a clean silicon wafer. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured on Nicolet 5700 spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA) in range of 400–4000 cm−1. The absorption spectra of aqueous solution of CQDs was measured on ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan) with different excitation wavelengths.

In order to investigate the influence of inorganic electrolyte on behaviors of CQDs, sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) and potassium nitrate (KNO3) were representatively used as electrolytes for electrochemical exfoliation. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), (b) and (c), the CQDs exfoliated in solution of NaCl, NaNO2 and Na2CO3 show similar behaviors on morphology with main size distribution of 0.5–3.5 nm and approximate size of 1.6 nm. Smaller size and narrower distribution of these CQDs may be due to weak oxidation and corrosion ability, leading to difficultly distinguish in the same resolution TEM images comparing with the CQDs exfoliated in nitrate electrolyte. While KNO3 as electrolyte, the exfoliated CQDs' average size is 3.3 nm and size distribution is in range of 1.5–6 nm, shown in Fig. 2 (d). Both results are basically consisted with the CQDs obtained in NaNO3, indicating that the type of anions determines the morphology of CQDs. The larger CQDs may be attribute to appearance of nitric acid during electrochemical exfoliation. Due to the applied voltage of 15 V is much greater than water decomposition voltage of 1.23 V, distilled water decomposes into hydrogen ions. The co-existence of nitrate and hydrogen ions in electrolyte will form nitrate acid, which can be evidenced by the pH of 3.1 in final electrolyte. Nitrate acid's strong oxidation and corrosivity behavior are favorable to the exfoliation, which is easier to obtain bigger CQDs. Besides, we can roughly tune the average size of CQDs by select the type of inorganic solution.

The formation mechanism and typical exfoliated process of graphite rods to exfoliated CQDs can be illustrated as following [24,31–34]. Due to applied voltage beyond 1.23 V, the distilled water in electrolyte decomposed into hydroxyl and oxygen radicals. A part radicals form hydrogen and oxygen bubbles on the surface of electrode, and anther potion radicals played a role in “scissoring” graphite rods owing to the hydroxylation and oxidation effect, which can exfoliate down CQDs from the rods. The “scissoring” process occurs initially at edge sites, grain boundaries, or defect sites. The sodium (or potassium) nitrate mainly played two roles in exfoliation process. On one hand, it significantly improved the conductivity of electrolyte, leading to accelerating the speed of dissociation and shorten reaction time. On the other hand, nitrate anion in electrolyte combined with hydrogen ion can formed nitric acid with strong oxidation ability, endowing this kind electrolyte with stronger “scissoring” ability than other inorganic salt solution. This enhanced oxidation ability makes it easier to break the original bond of graphite rods, and more bonds will be broken at once. Thus, there is a greater possibility to exfoliate off bigger CQDs from graphite rods. Besides, a wider distribution of size can provide additional evidence for this explanation.

To investigate the optical behaviors of CQDs exfoliated in different inorganic solution (NaNO3 (a), NaNO2 (b), NaCO3 (c) and KNO3(d)), the ultraviolet-visible (Uv-vis, right) and photoluminescence (PL, left) were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 , all exfoliated CQDs show a similarly characteristic absorption peaks located around 235 nm, which are attributed to π-π⁎ transition of CC from aromatic ring structure [35,36]. Besides, they resemble each other on PL behaviors, which show excitation-dependent on PL emission. The emission peaks of all CQDs red-shift to longer wavelengths from 450 to 580 nm, when the excitation wavelength vary from 340 to 520 nm with an increment of 20 nm. The peak intensity of emission reduces with an increase of excitation wavelength, and the strongest intensity of emission is excited by wavelength of 340 nm in all samples. The quantum yield of all CQDs is around 5.6%, obtained using a comparative method, in which quinine sulfate is used as standard sample [37]. These similarly PL properties may be ascribed to close environment of exfoliation, which lead to parallel PL center. Although many PL mechanisms are plausible, we are inclined to believe that PL originate from the quantum confinement effects due to few surface groups and low quantum yield (see next section).

To verify the chemical composition and the surface groups of the as-exfoliated CQDs, XPS and FT-IR spectra were characterized. Fig. 4a shows XPS survey spectrum of the CQDs with two obvious peaks of C1s (283.5 eV) and O1s (529.4 eV). A weak nitrogen peak (400.0 eV) and weak sodium peak at (494.1 eV) shown in the spectrum are attributed to the residual of NaNO3 on surface of CQDs. For the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b), there are three binding energy peaks of CC (282.0 eV), C-OH (283.2 eV) and CO (285.8 eV) [38,39]. The O1s spectrum of CQDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks of -OH (528.2 eV), CO (529.8 eV), and CO (533.2 eV) [40]. The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 4d) displays that the absorption band at 3458 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational absorption of –OH and the absorption band at 1637 cm−1 may be attributed to the vibration of aromatic CO [41]. The FT-IR results is consistent with the XPS results, in which -OH and CO are main and typical groups of CQDs. On basis of the above results, we can conclude that the oxygen functional groups are formed by electrochemical oxidation, and the sp2-hybridized CC in aromatic ring of graphite are partially decomposed into oxygen functional groups in the CQDs. These groups can efficiently improve the solubility of CQDs in water. However, comparing with current mainstream thermal carbonization (bottom-up) method, the process of electrochemical exfoliation generally forms some simple groups on CQDs surface by oxidation due to insufficient ability to retain or inherit groups from precursor molecules. The as-exfoliated CQDs with simple groups imply relatively complete graphite structure with less defects, which is of significance for electron transitions in composite materials. Besides, the simple groups of CQDs may be a reason of weaker PL emission, which is consisted with PL results.

The influence of applied voltages and concentrations on properties of the exfoliated CQDs was investigated as well. Fig. 5 show the TEM images and its size distribution (inset) of CQDs exfoliated at different concentration of 0.05 mol/L (a), 0.1 mol/L (b) and 0.5 mol/L (c), respectively. According to the fitting Gaussian curve (shown in the inset image), the as-exfoliated CQDs in 0.05 mol/L of sodium nitrate distribute in range of 0.8 to 5.8 nm with an average size of 3.14 nm. The ones exfoliated in 0.1 mol/L electrolyte have an average size of 3.07 nm, and the ones exfoliated in 0.5 mol/L electrolyte show the size of 3.29 nm. These CD exfoliated in different concentration of solution show similar diameter of around 3.15 nm and distribute in ranging of around 1 to 6 nm. There are no obvious changes in size and its distribution, which is different from our previous reports. It is well known that higher voltage or higher concentration will remarkably accelerate the speed of reaction owing to the enhancement of electricity in electrochemical system. However, the absence of big anion in electrolyte, like ions liquids, limit intercalating and expanding off CQDs with bigger size from graphite rods.

Herein, various inorganic solution was used as electrolyte to exfoliate CQDs from graphite bulk. The exfoliated CQDs in nitrate solution show easier distinguished morphology due to bigger average size of 3.15 nm, which is attributed to the excellent oxidation and corrosive of nitric acid formed in the process of electrochemical exfoliation. All CQDs exfoliated in inorganic solution possess similarly strong absorption in ultraviolet range and excitation-dependently PL properties. Although few surface groups limit PL of the CQDs, the relatively graphitic structure can improve carrier mobility, lead to better performance in optoelectronic applications. Based on above results and analysis, it can be concluded that nitrate may be the most optimal choice for the exfoliation of CQDs in inorganic electrolyte. Thus, we introduce a rapid, facile, and cheap approach for synthesis of the CQDs by electrochemical exfoliation from graphite bulk, which may be potentially applied in composite materials for catalysis and electronic devices.
Lijiang Tian, Xuehua Li and Xiaobing Li conceived and designed research.
Lijiang Tian, Fulong Chen and Hui Ding collected data and conducted research.
Lijiang Tian and Xuehua Li wrote the initial paper.
Fulong Chen, Xuehua Li and Xiaobing Li revised the paper.
All authors read and approved the final revised manuscript.
All authors declare that there are no competing interests.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51674261, No.51803233 No. 52004279), Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds and the Research Fund Program of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Development and Comprehensive Utilization of Mineral Resources (No. SK-201904). Jiangsu Province Key Research and Development Project (BE2018635) and Six Talents Peaks Project in Jiangsu Province (JNHB-077).
